name: NATO DIANA
overview: DIANA is NATO's start-up accelerator for deep-tech that matters to defence — it runs themed 'challenges', funds the winners with non-dilutive cash, and gives them access to a network of accelerator sites and test centres across the Alliance. AI runs through nearly everything it does, most visibly in challenges on decision-making, autonomy and intelligence/surveillance, and it sits alongside NATO's revised 2024 AI Strategy and the separate €1bn NATO Innovation Fund. Its public footprint is rich on cohorts, challenge topics, funding figures and named companies — the operational outcomes are mostly still ahead.

established.label: NATO's deep-tech accelerator
established.plain: DIANA is NATO's answer to 'how do we tap civilian start-ups for defence?'. Agreed at the 2021 Brussels Summit and formally launched at the 2022 Madrid Summit, it runs themed challenges, funds winners and connects them to military end-users. AI is one of its priority technology areas from the start.

established.label: The physical pipeline
established.plain: DIANA doesn't just hand out money — it plugs companies into a real network to mature and trial their tech. As of the 2026 cohort that means 16 accelerator sites and more than 200 test centres spread across the 32 NATO nations. This same test-centre network is what NATO's AI Strategy leans on for testing and validating AI.

established.label: The Alliance's AI plan, updated
established.plain: NATO refreshed its 2021 AI strategy in July 2024 to account for generative AI and AI-enabled information tools. It aims to speed up responsible AI adoption, keep Allied systems interoperable, protect NATO's own AI, and guard against adversaries' use of AI. Crucially, it points to DIANA's affiliated test centres as the backbone for testing and validating AI.
established.sourceName: NATO — Summary of NATO's revised AI strategy

established.label: The €1bn defence-tech VC
established.plain: Separate from DIANA but part of the same NATO innovation push, the €1bn NATO Innovation Fund (backed by 24 Allies) made its first direct investments in June 2024 — into ARX Robotics, Fractile, iCOMAT and Space Forge. Fractile is an explicitly AI company, building hardware to run very large AI models faster and cheaper.

established.label: 70+ companies, 2,600 bids
established.plain: From over 2,600 proposals, DIANA selected a 2025 cohort of around 70-plus companies headquartered across 20 NATO countries to work on ten challenge areas. This was the year the programme scaled up sharply and showed how competitive the funnel had become.
established.sourceName: NATO — Over 70 companies chosen for 2025 accelerator

established.label: 15 firms get €300k each
established.plain: In September 2025, DIANA down-selected 15 innovators from the 2025 cohort of 73 to move into phase two, each receiving €300,000 in non-dilutive funding plus continued test-centre access. The winners spanned cybersecurity, biotech, robotics, uncrewed vehicles, space and maritime defence.

inProgress.label: Largest-ever intake
inProgress.plain: Announced December 2025, DIANA's 2026 programme is its biggest yet: 150 companies from 24 NATO countries working on ten defence and security challenges, with the accelerator starting in January 2026. Several challenge areas — including data-assisted decision-making and autonomy/uncrewed systems — are heavily AI-driven.

inProgress.label: AI into the warfighting platform
inProgress.plain: An unusually mature DIANA challenge: instead of early prototypes it wants commercially ready software (TRL 7+) using AI, sensor fusion and space-based intelligence to speed up military planning. Winners get funding to integrate and demo their tool on NATO's AI-enabled warfighting platforms, including the Maven Smart System already used by Allied Command Operations. Submissions closed in May 2026.

inProgress.label: Six new challenges, AI inside
inProgress.plain: On 1 June 2026 DIANA opened applications for its 2027 cohort across six challenge areas — including 'Multidomain Sensing and Advanced Data Processing for Intelligence and Surveillance' and 'Multidomain Autonomy of Uncrewed Systems', both heavily AI/ISR-flavoured. Winners get €100,000 of contractual funding; applications close 3 July 2026 and the six-month accelerator starts January 2027.
inProgress.sourceName: Soldier Systems — DIANA unveils six 2027 challenges

inProgress.label: Proving AI is trustworthy
inProgress.plain: A named outcome of the 2024 AI Strategy: build an Alliance-wide landscape for testing and validating AI, using DIANA's affiliated test centres so AI systems can be checked against NATO's responsible-use principles before they're trusted. It's the connective tissue tying the strategy to DIANA's physical network.
inProgress.sourceName: NATO — Summary of NATO's revised AI strategy

latest.label: What NATO wants next
latest.plain: DIANA published its six challenge topics for 2027 in early June 2026: human survivability; multidomain autonomy of uncrewed systems; multidomain sensing and advanced data processing for intelligence and surveillance; operational resilience in contested environments; responsive logistics; and scalable, adaptable air-defence countermeasures. The intelligence/surveillance and autonomy themes are where AI matters most.
latest.sourceName: Soldier Systems — DIANA unveils six 2027 challenges

latest.label: Bids in, integration ahead
latest.plain: The window for the AI/ISR 'Decision Superiority' challenge closed on 5 May 2026. Selected companies are to integrate and demonstrate their software on NATO's deployed AI warfighting platforms — a rare case of DIANA pushing mature AI straight toward operational use rather than experimentation.

suppliers.name: Fractile
suppliers.role: AI hardware start-up — first direct investment by the NATO Innovation Fund (chips to run large AI models faster/cheaper)
suppliers.claim: "We're thrilled to have raised our funding from investors with a wealth of experience in the AI and chip industries." — Walter Goodwin, CEO

suppliers.name: ARX Robotics
suppliers.role: Defence robotics/autonomy firm — NATO Innovation Fund first direct investment
suppliers.claim: "The demand for modular, software-driven defence systems is growing rapidly — and we're building the company that will define this category in Europe." — Marc Wietfeld, co-founder & CEO

suppliers.name: Wayren (Estonia)
suppliers.claim: "Being selected for DIANA Phase 2 is a huge vote of confidence in our mission to ensure mission-critical teams stay operational when it matters most." — Henry Härm, CEO
suppliers.sourceName: Defence Industry Europe — Wayren in DIANA

suppliers.name: Alea Quantum (Denmark)
suppliers.claim: "Alea Quantum Technologies and Celare Quantum Communications presented our quantum encryption technology in front of more than 600 attendees for the NATO DIANA Security Days in Munich."

sources.name: NATO — Summary of NATO's revised AI strategy (2024)

unknowns: Which specific companies won the AI-heavy 'Decision Superiority' challenge, and the operational results of integrating their tools onto NATO's warfighting platforms, are not yet public. DIANA does not publicly break out exactly how many of its cohort companies are pure-AI versus AI-adjacent, and most 2026 cohort firms are unnamed in the public announcements.
